title: 911 SAVES Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill tells the government to think about giving 911 operators their own special job code when they update how they group different types of jobs. Right now, 911 operators might be grouped with other jobs, but this bill says they should be put in the group with police and firefighters since their work helps protect people. The bill says 911 operators do much more than just answer phones and pass along messages, and their job can be very stressful because they help save lives. If the government decides not to give 911 operators their own job code, they have to write a report to Congress explaining why they said no.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Supporting Accurate Views of Emergency Services Act of 2025 or the 911 SAVES Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts about public safety telecommunicators (the people who answer 911 calls and talk to police, firefighters, and emergency medical workers). These workers play a very important role in emergency response. Their job is much more than just passing messages between the public and first responders. When children are reported missing, kidnapped, or being hurt, the information that 911 operators get and the actions they take form the foundation for helping those children. When someone who has taken hostages or wants to hurt themselves calls 911, the operator is the first person they talk to. The operator's ability to talk them through the situation can stop things from getting worse. During shootings where someone is attacking many people, 911 operators help callers give first aid and stay safe while also getting important information to help police officers who are responding. When police officers, firefighters, and emergency medical workers are being shot at, they call 911 operators for help. These operators often talk to people who are very upset, scared, hurt, or in danger. They use their experience and training to pick out important information that could save lives. There have been cases where 911 operators heard the sound of someone loading a shotgun and warned police officers so they would not walk into a dangerous trap. This work causes serious emotional and physical stress that gets worse because of long work hours and the fact that emergencies happen all day and night. Research shows that 911 operators are exposed to trauma that may cause them to develop posttraumatic stress disorder (a mental health condition that can happen after experiencing or witnessing scary events). Some agencies provide special counseling teams to help reduce the mental impact and help 911 operators and first responders recover faster. The Standard Occupational Classification system is designed and used only for keeping statistics. Federal agencies use it to put workers and jobs into categories so they can collect, calculate, study, and share information about different types of work. Jobs in this system are grouped based on the work people do and sometimes on the skills, education, or training needed to do that work. Putting 911 operators in the Protective Service Occupations category would fix an incorrect classification, recognize these professionals for their lifesaving work, and make the system match better with other similar classification systems.

sections.title: Section 3 Revision of Standard Occupational Classification System
sections.text: The Director of the Office of Management and Budget (called the Director in this law) must consider creating a separate job code for public safety telecommunicators as part of protective service occupations. This must happen during the first time the Standard Occupational Classification system gets updated after this law is passed. If the Director decides not to create this separate code for 911 operators, the Director must write a report to Congress explaining why. This report must be sent to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs of the Senate and the Committee on Education and the Workforce of the House of Representatives within sixty days after the Director announces the final decision in the Federal Register (the official daily publication of the federal government).
